name: Balmung
description: Balmung is the magical sword wielded by the hero Siegfried (Sigurd) in Germanic mythology, most famously in the Nibelungenlied and the Volsunga Saga. Forged by the legendary dwarf smith Regin, Balmung was initially owned by Siegfried's father, Sigmund, who received it from Odin himself during a battle. The sword was broken in that battle but later reforged by Regin for Siegfried. Balmung is described as being incredibly sharp and strong, capable of cleaving through anything, including anvils and even the dragon Fafnir. It grants Siegfried immense power and invincibility, playing a vital role in his dragon-slaying and subsequent adventures. The sword is a symbol of heroism, strength, and destiny, but also carries a tragic fate, ultimately contributing to Siegfried's downfall. Its existence is intertwined with themes of greed, betrayal, and the corrupting influence of power. Balmung's sharpness and magical properties are often attributed to its creation by a skilled dwarf and its association with divine power. The sword is not just a weapon; it is a legendary artifact embodying the hero's journey and the complex moral landscape of Germanic myth.
subtitle: Siegfried's legendary and powerful sword
origin: Forged by Regin for Siegfried, originally owned by Sigmund and gifted by Odin
currentLocation: Unknown, lost after Siegfried's death or returned to the Rhine

keyMyths.title: The Reforging of Balmung
keyMyths.description: Regin reforges the shattered sword Balmung for Siegfried, after the hero proves his strength and courage. The reforged sword is tested against an anvil, which it cleaves in two.

keyMyths.title: Siegfried Slays Fafnir
keyMyths.description: Siegfried uses Balmung to slay the dragon Fafnir, who guards a vast treasure hoard. The sword proves its worth by piercing the dragon's scales and heart, securing Siegfried's victory.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: A 13th-century Icelandic prose saga which tells the story of the Volsung clan, including Sigmund and Sigurd (Siegfried).
